critique.summary: Meta announced that starting in 2026, the Horizon Worlds VR app will be removed from Meta Quest devices, transitioning Horizon Worlds to a mobile-only platform. Several Horizon Worlds features and perks will be discontinued or moved, while users can still access some VR worlds until mid-2026. The update aims to streamline the Meta Quest experience but has received mixed reactions from the community.
mainPoints: Horizon Worlds and Events will no longer be available on Meta Quest VR after June 15, 2026.
Certain popular Horizon Worlds like Horizon Central, Events Arena, Kaiju, and Bobber Bay will be removed from VR by March 31, 2026.
Horizon Worlds will continue as a mobile-only experience via the Meta Horizon mobile app.
Hyperscape Capture (Beta) viewing is moving out of Horizon Worlds by March 24, 2026, with limited sharing capabilities thereafter.
Horizon-specific perks in the Meta Horizon Plus subscription will be removed by March 31, 2026, but core gaming benefits remain.
Meta is continuing to improve the Quest experience with new features and interface updates.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with the official announcement from Meta's community forums and official Meta sources. The timeline and details about the discontinuation of Horizon Worlds on Quest VR and the shift to mobile-only align with Meta's publicly stated plans. User comments reflect genuine community reactions but do not contradict the factual information. There is no evidence of misinformation or false claims in the article.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential impact on users who rely on VR for social interaction and community building.
There is no mention of alternative VR platforms or solutions for displaced Horizon Worlds users.
The article does not provide data or rationale from Meta explaining the business or technical reasons behind the decision.
Potential effects on content creators and developers within Horizon Worlds VR are not explored.
User experience differences between the VR and mobile versions of Horizon Worlds are only briefly touched upon in comments, not in the main article.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Meta's discontinuation of Horizon Worlds on Meta Quest, with commenters expressing disappointment over wasted investment, mismanagement, and strategic missteps. Many highlight the challenges of pioneering the metaverse early, the potential layoffs, and question Meta's leadership and future direction.

comments.author: atrus
comments.text:
Despite the shock at the amount of money meta spent on their version of the metaverse, I don't think they spent nearly enough to accomplish their vision.

Meta, to the detriment of the market, tried too early in the VR lifecycle to own the market. They basically tried to become the iPhone and Apple in the year 1990.

Tell me, do you believe any singular company in the year 1990, with 100B to burn, would be able to create the iPhone, in any of its varations? Absolutely not, there too much research, too much to invent, too much to program and not nearly enough talent and money for one company to manage.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on the timing and scale of Meta's metaverse ambitions, accurately highlighting the challenges of pioneering new technology markets.

comments.author: paxys
comments.text: It’s wild how much mismanagement Zuck is able to get away with just because he controls majority voting shares in Meta. At this point all of the missteps by the company in AR/VR, AI and everything else can directly be traced back to him. Half-baked vision, massive spending and no accountability. The company desperately needs a Sundar/Satya type leader.
comments.analysis: The critique of leadership and accountability is supported by Meta's recent strategic failures, making this a credible and insightful comment.

comments.author: t-writescode
comments.text:
They were trying to compete with an existing, VERY good couple of alternatives, and the people most actually likely to use that product were already on those services.

It was a losing play that didn’t know what market it was actually entering.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the competitive landscape and market confusion that likely contributed to Horizon Worlds' failure.

comments.author: tyleo
comments.text:
Interesting, cutting way back in the product they renamed the whole company for.

They feel a bit directionless to me. They are still making money but even their AI attempt feels half hearted. I think they are really trying but I’m not sure they can build the engineering muscle to move in new areas with the brand damage they’ve sustained.
comments.analysis: The observation about brand damage and lack of clear direction aligns with recent Meta challenges, though it is somewhat speculative.

comments.author: xd1936
comments.text: Unbelievable. They re-architected the whole operating system around this stupid app. They discontinued their previous homescreen environments in favor of trying to promote Horizon Worlds, only to discontinue the blasted thing anyway? After all of those millions of dollars spent trying to make virtual events happen?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration but correctly notes the significant investment and strategic pivot that now seems wasted.

comments.author: drivebyhooting
comments.text: When will horizon worlds be axed entirely? It has something like fewer than 10,000 active users at any given time.
comments.analysis: The low user count is a factual point that supports the rationale for discontinuation.

comments.author: jesse_dot_id
comments.text:
I never understood why they were trying to recreate real life social interactions in VR, because it's worse by default, and the majority of the nerds who buy this tech are probably trying to escape that on some level. I know that any time I went into Meta Horizon Worlds, I didn't want to hear 95% of the people I heard talking.

What I do use VR for is Bigscreen VR nearly every night to watch stuff with my friends. Scrolling through reels in a movie theater is pretty fun and even though I never do it solo on my phone, I will sit there for like 3-4 hours in VR enjoying communal brain rot.

Perhaps they should focus on things like that instead of gimmicks that nobody cares about. For example, I have never once played a game in VR that didn't force me to sit or stand in a specific position, meaning to play it, I have to go out of my way to do so.
comments.analysis: Provides a user perspective on VR usage preferences, suggesting alternative successful VR applications.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: Maybe I'm just naive but I don't really understand discontinuing things like this. Like, unless there are like 100 people using this, how can it not be possible to just leave this running at like 0.5% of its former capacity. Just leave up like 1 server, collapse all of the DBs into one, and let these few autists have their stuff.
comments.analysis: Raises a practical question about resource allocation post-discontinuation, reflecting common industry practices.

comments.author: bhouston
comments.text:
Okay, how long until Meta Quest is discontinued/sunset?

I believe there is no expectation of a Meta Quest 4 right?
comments.analysis: Speculates on hardware lifecycle based on current trends, a reasonable inference though not confirmed.

comments.author: jeffbee
comments.text: Second Life, which contains only niche perverts, has more concurrent actives than Horizon Worlds.
comments.analysis: This comparison highlights Horizon Worlds' low engagement relative to established virtual worlds, supporting the argument of its failure.
